title: Extrinsic quantum geometry in the quadrupolar bulk photovoltaic effect
authors: Steven Gassner, Swati Chaudhary, Martin Claassen
abstract: The bulk photovoltaic effect has become a valuable probe of the quantum geometry of Bloch bands. While it is restricted to inversion-broken systems within the dipole approximation, the finite momentum of light is appreciated to give rise to this effect even in centrosymmetric crystals, an effect referred to as "photon drag." In this work, we recast the photon drag effect at leading order in the optical wavevector, highlighting a previously neglected contribution arising intuitively from the electric quadrupole correction to light-matter coupling. In the language of band geometry, we identify this interband quadrupole as a multiband metric tensor that quantifies the variation of two resonantly driven states extrinsic to the subspace they span. We predict that systems in which three or more bands strongly admix in momentum space, such as twisted MoTe$_2$ bilayers, will have anomalously large photon drag due to this quadrupolar effect. Our work provides a conceptual bridge between band-geometric organizing principles and electromagnetic multipole corrections in nonlinear optics.
summary: This theoretical work develops a framework to calculate the bulk photovoltaic effect using an expansion sensitive to higher-order electric quadrupole contributions. By analyzing these 'extrinsic' geometric terms, the authors predict that materials with complex band mixing, such as twisted bilayer semiconductors, will exhibit enhanced photocurrents measurable via photon drag effects.
main_problem: The work investigates the role of 'extrinsic quantum geometry' in the quadrupolar bulk photovoltaic effect (BPVE), extending beyond standard dipole approximations.
main_result: The authors predict that systems with strong multi-band mixing, such as twisted MoTe2 bilayers, will exhibit anomalously large photon drag due to this higher-order quadrupolar effect.
method: The analysis uses diagrammatic perturbation theory and expands the second-order optical conductivity ($\sigma_{\mu\alpha eta}^{PV}$) to linear order in the light wavevector ($\mathbf{q}$), separating contributions into multipolar terms.
model_or_system: The study is based on continuum models of electronic band structures, particularly applied to twisted bilayer materials like MoTe2. The theoretical framework involves Bloch Hamiltonians and calculating current operator matrix elements.
key_observables: Photovoltaic conductivity ($\sigma_{\mu\alpha eta}^{PV}$), which is proportional to the electric quadrupole moment ($eQ$) when expanded in $\mathbf{q}$.
important_parameters: The analysis focuses on the linear-in-$\mathbf{q}$ correction, comparing results across different multipolar contributions (dipole vs. quadrupole).
main_assumptions: Key assumptions include expanding responses linearly in $\mathbf{q}$ and utilizing approximations like the noninteracting electron picture within diagrammatic calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the coupling of interband dipole versus quadrupole moments to light-matter interaction, and show calculated shift currents for minimal three-band models and twisted MoTe2.
paper_structure: The paper builds from general theoretical derivations of $\sigma_{\mu\alpha eta}^{PV}$ using diagrammatic techniques (Diagram 1 & 2), systematically derives the linear-$\mathbf{q}$ expansion, and applies this formalism to specific physical systems like twisted bilayer MoTe2.
why_interesting: The explicit connection drawn between band-geometric quantities (like extrinsic quadrupole moments) and measurable electromagnetic multipole corrections in nonlinear optics provides a powerful new diagnostic tool for characterizing electronic structure beyond simple dipole responses.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electric quadrupole corrections to optical responses encode extrinsic quantum geometry. (a) Electrons in Bloch bands couple to a uniform electric field via an interband elec- tric dipole matrix element. Gradients in the electric field cou- ple to an interband quadrupole matrix element. (b) The in- terband electric dipole rµ mnk probes the intrinsic geometry of the Bloch states, i.e. within the resonant two-band mani- fold. (c) The origin-independent part of the interband electric quadrupole, which we denote as eQνµ mnk, probes the extrinsic geometry of the Bloch states, i.e. orthogonal to the resonant two-band manifold. FIG. 3. Oblique-incidence photocurrents probe the extrin- sic quantum geometry of the moir´e bands in tMoTe2. (a) Schematic of a photocurrent experiment in a twisted bilayer material whereby oblique incidence introduces an in-plane component to the optical wavevector. (b) The yxyy compo- nent of the real linear-in-wavevector photovoltaic conductivity (for which the magnetic dipole contribution is forbidden by symmetry) computed from continuum models of valley-and- spin-polarized tMoTe2 at three different twist angles. The considerably larger response for the twist angle where the top few bands have equal Chern number can be anticipated from the viewpoint of extrinsic quantum geometry.
